title: Investigating pedestrian suggestions for external features on fully autonomous vehicles: A virtual reality experiment
authors: Shuchisnigdha Deb; Lesley J. Strawderman; Daniel W. Carruth
research_problem: Identify potential external features on fully autonomous vehicles (FAVs) that would help pedestrians understand vehicle intended behavior at crosswalks, improve receptivity toward FAVs, and influence crossing behavior
method_summary: Virtual reality experiment with 30 participants walking across a virtual crosswalk in front of a FAV. Tested 4 visual features (walking silhouette, 'braking' in text, flashing smile, no signal) and 4 audible features (horn sound, music, verbal message, no signal) in a 4x4 randomized complete block design. Collected simulator data (crossing time, waiting time, head rotation) and survey data (receptivity surveys, pedestrian behavior questionnaire, personal innovativeness scale, feature ratings)
method_family: Virtual Reality Experiment, Randomized Complete Block Design
tasks: Pedestrian crossing behavior at crosswalks with FAVs; Feature preference evaluation; Receptivity assessment toward autonomous vehicles
metrics: Feature ratings (1-7 scale); Crossing time; Waiting time; Pedestrian Receptivity Questionnaire for FAVs (PRQF) scores; Pedestrian Behavior Questionnaire (PBQ) scores; Personal innovativeness scale scores; Simulation Sickness Questionnaire (SSQ) scores
claim_text: External features on FAVs significantly increased pedestrian receptivity toward autonomous vehicles
limitations: Single controlled scenario with FAV approaching from one direction and stopping at intersection; no other traffic in virtual environment | Features indicated safe crossing conditions only; vehicle stopped in every trial - did not test negative or terminating signals | First-time exposure to FAV may not reflect long-term behavior and adaptation | Small sample size (n=30) limits generalizability; conducted at single university with U.S. citizens only
notes: VR platform validated by previous study (Deb, Carruth, et al., 2017) confirmed pedestrians respond similarly in virtual and real traffic environments; External features selected based on previous research, development feasibility, and pedestrians' ability to interpret without prior training; Participants had normal or corrected full-color vision, normal hearing, and normal walking gait; Personal innovativeness negatively correlated with waiting time - higher innovativeness led to shorter waiting times before crossing

authors: Shuchisnigdha Deb; Lesley J. Strawderman; Daniel W. Carruth
research_problem: Identify potential external features on fully autonomous vehicles (FAVs) that would help pedestrians understand vehicle intended behavior at crosswalks, improve receptivity toward FAVs, and influence crossing behavior

notes: VR platform validated by previous study (Deb, Carruth, et al., 2017) confirmed pedestrians respond similarly in virtual and real traffic environments; External features selected based on previous research, development feasibility, and pedestrians' ability to interpret without prior training; Participants had normal or corrected full-color vision, normal hearing, and normal walking gait; Personal innovativeness negatively correlated with waiting time - higher innovativeness led to shorter waiting times before crossing

title: Exploring factors affecting pedestrians' red-light running behaviors at intersections in China
authors: Weihua Zhang; Kun Wang; Lei Wang; Zhongxiang Feng; Yingjie Du
research_problem: Exploring factors affecting pedestrians' red-light running behaviors at intersections in China, using data collected from Hefei
method_summary: SP survey with 15 items collecting pedestrian socio-economic characteristics, trip related features, and attitude variables. Based on 631 valid samples, a binomial logistic model was established using Score test and iterative variable selection to evaluate impacts of contributing factors on pedestrian red-light running behavior.
tasks: analyze factors affecting pedestrian red-light running behavior at signalized intersections
datasets: 631 valid survey samples from 6 signalized intersections in Hefei, China (May-June 2015)
metrics: Score test, coefficients, odds ratios, p-values, 95% confidence intervals
claim_text: Trip purpose significantly affects red-light running behavior - strolling pedestrians are more likely to have safe crossing behavior compared to those going to work/school
limitations: Data collected only from one Chinese city (Hefei); results may not generalize to other cities or countries due to cultural and driving behavior differences | Study only considered pedestrian red-light running at signalized intersection areas; other crossing facility types not examined | Study focused on individual crossing behaviors, ignoring impacts of pedestrian platoons and herd mentality effects | Sample size of 631 may limit generalizability despite being sufficient for model estimation
notes: Four significant variables identified: trip purpose, time period, attitude towards running red-light when in hurry, attitude towards road facility quality affecting crossing behavior; Model uses binomial logistic regression with Score test for variable selection; Field survey conducted during peak periods on weekdays and weekends; Voice reminder and monitoring equipment suggested as countermeasures

title: Exploring factors affecting pedestrians' red-light running behaviors at intersections in China

tasks: analyze factors affecting pedestrian red-light running behavior at signalized intersections
datasets: 631 valid survey samples from 6 signalized intersections in Hefei, China (May-June 2015)
metrics: Score test, coefficients, odds ratios, p-values, 95% confidence intervals
claim_text: Time period in a day significantly affects red-light running probability, with daytime having the highest probability (OR=3.326) compared to dawn
limitations: Data collected only from one Chinese city (Hefei); results may not generalize to other cities or countries due to cultural and driving behavior differences | Study only considered pedestrian red-light running at signalized intersection areas; other crossing facility types not examined | Study focused on individual crossing behaviors, ignoring impacts of pedestrian platoons and herd mentality effects | Sample size of 631 may limit generalizability despite being sufficient for model estimation
notes: Four significant variables identified: trip purpose, time period, attitude towards running red-light when in hurry, attitude towards road facility quality affecting crossing behavior; Model uses binomial logistic regression with Score test for variable selection; Field survey conducted during peak periods on weekdays and weekends; Voice reminder and monitoring equipment suggested as countermeasures

authors: Weihua Zhang; Kun Wang; Lei Wang; Zhongxiang Feng; Yingjie Du
research_problem: Exploring factors affecting pedestrians' red-light running behaviors at intersections in China, using data collected from Hefei

datasets: 631 valid survey samples from 6 signalized intersections in Hefei, China (May-June 2015)
metrics: Score test, coefficients, odds ratios, p-values, 95% confidence intervals
claim_text: Pedestrians who indicate they will run red-light when in hurry have significantly higher probability of such behavior (OR=0.299 for non-hurry group)
limitations: Data collected only from one Chinese city (Hefei); results may not generalize to other cities or countries due to cultural and driving behavior differences | Study only considered pedestrian red-light running at signalized intersection areas; other crossing facility types not examined | Study focused on individual crossing behaviors, ignoring impacts of pedestrian platoons and herd mentality effects | Sample size of 631 may limit generalizability despite being sufficient for model estimation
notes: Four significant variables identified: trip purpose, time period, attitude towards running red-light when in hurry, attitude towards road facility quality affecting crossing behavior; Model uses binomial logistic regression with Score test for variable selection; Field survey conducted during peak periods on weekdays and weekends; Voice reminder and monitoring equipment suggested as countermeasures

title: Exploring factors affecting pedestrians' red-light running behaviors at intersections in China
authors: Weihua Zhang; Kun Wang; Lei Wang; Zhongxiang Feng; Yingjie Du
research_problem: Exploring factors affecting pedestrians' red-light running behaviors at intersections in China, using data collected from Hefei
method_summary: SP survey with 15 items collecting pedestrian socio-economic characteristics, trip related features, and attitude variables. Based on 631 valid samples, a binomial logistic model was established using Score test and iterative variable selection to evaluate impacts of contributing factors on pedestrian red-light running behavior.
tasks: analyze factors affecting pedestrian red-light running behavior at signalized intersections
datasets: 631 valid survey samples from 6 signalized intersections in Hefei, China (May-June 2015)
metrics: Score test, coefficients, odds ratios, p-values, 95% confidence intervals
claim_text: Pedestrians whose crossing behavior is affected by road facility quality are more likely to run red-lights at intersections (OR=0.483 for unaffected group)
limitations: Data collected only from one Chinese city (Hefei); results may not generalize to other cities or countries due to cultural and driving behavior differences | Study only considered pedestrian red-light running at signalized intersection areas; other crossing facility types not examined | Study focused on individual crossing behaviors, ignoring impacts of pedestrian platoons and herd mentality effects | Sample size of 631 may limit generalizability despite being sufficient for model estimation
notes: Four significant variables identified: trip purpose, time period, attitude towards running red-light when in hurry, attitude towards road facility quality affecting crossing behavior; Model uses binomial logistic regression with Score test for variable selection; Field survey conducted during peak periods on weekdays and weekends; Voice reminder and monitoring equipment suggested as countermeasures

title: Roles of personal and environmental factors in the red light running propensity of pedestrian: Case study at the urban crosswalks
authors: Dianchen Zhu; N.N. Sze; Lu Bai
research_problem: Identify factors that affect the propensity of red light running of pedestrians at signalized intersections, including both personal factors (pedestrian demographics and behavior) and environmental factors (presence and behavior of other pedestrians, signal time, and traffic condition)
method_summary: Video observation surveys conducted at six signalized crosswalks in urban Hong Kong, capturing crossing behaviors of 6320 pedestrians during 652 signal cycles. Random parameter logit model developed to measure association between red light running propensity and explanatory factors, accounting for unobserved heterogeneity
method_family: Random parameter logit model (discrete choice model)
tasks: Pedestrian red light running violation prediction; Factor analysis of pedestrian crossing behavior
datasets: 6320 pedestrians observed at 6 signalized crosswalks in Hong Kong; 652 signal cycles; Six locations: Prince Edward Road West j/w Nathan Road, Argyle Street j/w Nathan Road, Argyle Street j/w Sai Yee Street, Tonkin Street j/w Shun Ning Road, Hung Hom South Road j/w Po loi Street, To Kwa Wan Road j/w Chi Kiang Street
metrics: Red light running violation (binary outcome); Odds ratios; Elasticities; AIC (Akaike Information Criterion) for model comparison; Z-values for statistical significance
claim_text: Pedestrian gender, age, number of lanes, presence of a companion, number of pedestrians around, presence of other violators in the same cycle, time to green, red time, traffic volume, and percentage of heavy vehicles all significantly affect the propensity of red light running violation of pedestrians
limitations: Study focuses on red light running propensity but does not analyze safety outcomes (associated crash risk) | Effect of vehicular speed on red light running behavior was not assessed despite its correlation with crash risk and severity | Video observation survey cannot capture pedestrian safety perception, attitude, or anticipated crossing behavior that could be gauged through questionnaire or attitudinal surveys | Limited to urban Hong Kong context; findings may not generalize to other cities with different traffic signal systems (e.g., pedestrian countdown displays)
notes: Study addresses gap in literature by examining both individual-level (personal demographics and behavior) and cycle-level (traffic condition and signal time) factors together; Novel contribution includes examining influence of social norms through presence of companion, number of pedestrians around, and violation behavior of others; Hong Kong pedestrian signal cycle has three phases: steady green, flashing green, and steady red (no countdown display); Random parameter approach justified by statistically significant standard deviation components indicating unobserved heterogeneity in gender and number of people waiting effects; 18.5% of observed pedestrians (1169 out of 6320) committed red light running violation

title: Roles of personal and environmental factors in the red light running propensity of pedestrian: Case study at the urban crosswalks
authors: Dianchen Zhu; N.N. Sze; Lu Bai
research_problem: Identify factors that affect the propensity of red light running of pedestrians at signalized intersections, including both personal factors (pedestrian demographics and behavior) and environmental factors (presence and behavior of other pedestrians, signal time, and traffic condition)
method_summary: Video observation surveys conducted at six signalized crosswalks in urban Hong Kong, capturing crossing behaviors of 6320 pedestrians during 652 signal cycles. Random parameter logit model developed to measure association between red light running propensity and explanatory factors, accounting for unobserved heterogeneity
method_family: Random parameter logit model (discrete choice model)
tasks: Pedestrian red light running violation prediction; Factor analysis of pedestrian crossing behavior
datasets: 6320 pedestrians observed at 6 signalized crosswalks in Hong Kong; 652 signal cycles; Six locations: Prince Edward Road West j/w Nathan Road, Argyle Street j/w Nathan Road, Argyle Street j/w Sai Yee Street, Tonkin Street j/w Shun Ning Road, Hung Hom South Road j/w Po loi Street, To Kwa Wan Road j/w Chi Kiang Street
metrics: Red light running violation (binary outcome); Odds ratios; Elasticities; AIC (Akaike Information Criterion) for model comparison; Z-values for statistical significance
claim_text: Female pedestrians have lower propensity of red light running compared to males; given normally distributed gender parameter, probability that females have lower propensity than males is 64.3%
limitations: Study focuses on red light running propensity but does not analyze safety outcomes (associated crash risk) | Effect of vehicular speed on red light running behavior was not assessed despite its correlation with crash risk and severity | Video observation survey cannot capture pedestrian safety perception, attitude, or anticipated crossing behavior that could be gauged through questionnaire or attitudinal surveys | Limited to urban Hong Kong context; findings may not generalize to other cities with different traffic signal systems (e.g., pedestrian countdown displays)
notes: Study addresses gap in literature by examining both individual-level (personal demographics and behavior) and cycle-level (traffic condition and signal time) factors together; Novel contribution includes examining influence of social norms through presence of companion, number of pedestrians around, and violation behavior of others; Hong Kong pedestrian signal cycle has three phases: steady green, flashing green, and steady red (no countdown display); Random parameter approach justified by statistically significant standard deviation components indicating unobserved heterogeneity in gender and number of people waiting effects; 18.5% of observed pedestrians (1169 out of 6320) committed red light running violation

authors: Dianchen Zhu; N.N. Sze; Lu Bai
